full_response:
We label the two inertial frames as  
• R  (unprimed coordinates)  
• R′  (primed coordinates)  

R′ moves with constant velocity  
v = v eₓ  with v = 3c/5 ⇒ β ≡ v/c = 3/5 = 0.6.  

The origins coincide at t = t′ = 0, and the x–axes are parallel, so the standard Lorentz transformation (boost along x) applies.

Step 1:  Compute γ  
γ = 1/√(1 − β²) = 1 / √(1 − (3/5)²)  
         = 1 / √(1 − 9/25) = 1 / √(16/25) = 1 / (4/5) = 5/4 = 1.25.

Step 2:  Write the Lorentz transformation (c = 1 units)  
ct′ = γ (ct − β x)  
x′  = γ (x − β ct)  
y′  = y  
z′  = z.

Step 3:  Insert the coordinates of event A in frame R  
Event A: (ct, x, y, z) = (1, 2, 3, 4).

• Time coordinate in R′  
ct′ = γ (ct − β x) = 1.25 [ 1 − 0.6 (2) ]  
        = 1.25 ( 1 − 1.2 ) = 1.25 ( −0.2 ) = −0.25.

• Spatial x–coordinate in R′  
x′ = γ ( x − β ct ) = 1.25 [ 2 − 0.6 (1) ]  
    = 1.25 ( 2 − 0.6 ) = 1.25 ( 1.4 ) = 1.75.

• Transverse coordinates are unchanged  
y′ = y = 3, z′ = z = 4.

Step 4:  Assemble the primed 4-vector  
(ct′, x′, y′, z′) = ( −0.25, 1.75, 3, 4 ).

If one prefers to display the ordinary time coordinate explicitly, t′ = ct′/c = −0.25/c; in units with c = 1, ct′ = −0.25 suffices.

\boxed{(ct',\,x',\,y',\,z') = (-0.25,\;1.75,\;3,\;4)}

Confidence: 93%
